description: One of three rivers called "Salsum" by the Romans, this river is probably to be identified with the modern Sous, which debouches into the Atlantic Ocean at modern Agadir in Morocco.

history.comment: added Wikldata, Geonames, and TGN references provided by @jbecker; summary by @thomase; Names and locations by @thomase and by @enesy

names.details: Nehr-i Nīl (Nile River): ... After this, from the Nīl-i Mıṣr (Nile of Egypt), the Nehr-i Feyyūm (Fayyum River / Bahr Yussef Canal) branches off. When its waters increase, it reaches the Nehr-i Sūs-i Aḳṣā (the Far Sūs River / possibly the Oued Souss in Morocco). The Nehr-i Sūs (Souss River) descends from the Cebel-i Lemṭa. It passes through the city of Sūs (Souss) and flows north of it. On its shores grow abundant sugarcane (ḳaṣab-ı sükker) and henna (ḥınnā). That is, just as the produce of the regions around the Nile is obtained from it, so too is produce taken from the River Sūs. Afterwards, it flows into the sea. [partial translation]

references.formattedCitation: Âli, Mustafa bin Ahmet. Künhü’l-ahbâr: (tenkitli metin). Edited by Suat Donuk and Derya Örs. 1. baskı. 5 vols. Türkiye Yazma Eserler Kurumu Başkanlığı yayınları. Fatih, İstanbul: Türkiye Yazma Eserler Kurumu Başkanlığı, 2020. https://ekitap.yek.gov.tr/urun/kunhu%E2%80%99l-ahbar--takim-5-cilt-_821.aspx.
